title: Why PlausiDen exists
description: Software written today should outlive its dependencies. The platform behind that claim.

sections.title: We engineer for the next thousand years.
sections.lede: Not because we expect software written today to literally run for a millennium — but because the discipline of designing as though it should produces fundamentally better software right now.

sections.text: Most stacks ask you to pick two or three axes from the list — fast or anonymous, reliable or private. PlausiDen refuses the tradeoff. Every architectural decision scores against all six at once:

items.hint: Static-first, audit-gated, no runtime database for content.
Every commit produces a deterministic artifact + signed audit report.
If the types compile, the system doesn't break at runtime.
Gated at build time, not bolted on after.

items.value: Multi-network publish (Tor, I2P, IPFS, Gemini, Lokinet)
items.hint: Your audience picks the network — not us.
We keep no copy of your content. The platform respects the user's data class.

sections.text: Most AI platforms put the LLM at the center and audit its output retroactively. PlausiDen inverts that. The LLM is a constrained candidate generator. The Lattice of Formal Inference — LFI — is the upstream evaluator. Every AI proposal flows through a typed Critic that returns Accept, Reject, or Refine. There is no code path where an LLM's output reaches the commit boundary without that gate. It's a type signature, not a code-review policy.

sections.body: We weren't trying to make AI go away. We were trying to make AI accountable. The Critic trait is what makes that possible — it's the seam where 'the model said so' becomes 'these specific rules fired with these specific weights.'

sections.text: WordPress, Contentful, Sanity, Strapi — they all store content in a database, render through a template language, and validate at publish time. The result: 60% of CMS migration cost is fixing content that the template-language compiler couldn't catch. PlausiDen CMS treats content as a typed value. Your pages are JSON. JSON deserialises to closed-enum Rust variants. The compiler catches missing alt-text and broken slugs at the boundary — before the publish, before the deploy, before the user.

A static site is a Merkle tree of bytes. It can't have a SQL injection. It can't have a row-level permission bug. It can't accidentally ship a debug logger to production. The cost of static-first is dynamic-feature friction; the cost of dynamic-first is the cumulative attack surface of every runtime your stack imports. PlausiDen makes the static path cheap and the dynamic path opt-in.

The clearnet is one network. Tor is another. IPFS is another. Gemini, I2P, Lokinet — others still. PlausiDen treats every network as a typed DeployAdapter with a SecurityProfile projection. Your audience picks the network. You publish once, the platform packages for each adapter's constraints automatically. The web isn't a single substrate; we stopped pretending it was.

sections.title: Read the docs.
sections.lede: PlausiDen is in beta. The contracts are stable; the runtime is hardening. If you're building something you want to ship in five years and still own in twenty, this is for you.
